coredata.dc:title: Preparation, characterization of naringenin, β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dot antioxidant nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Liang, Yan
Hou, Dongyuan
Ni, Zan
Cao, Minjie
Cai, Luyun
coredata.dc:description: In this work, naringenin loaded β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dots composite nanoparticles were successfully fabricated. The results showed that incorporation of carbon quantum dots not only enhanced antioxidant activities of nanoparticles but also improved encapsulation efficiency of naringenin. Further, the formation of composite nanoparticles was confirmed by a series of characterization methods. The zeta-potential and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy data proved that electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding are dominant forces to form nanoparticles. X-Ray Diffraction experiment revealed that the material state of the formed naringenin-β-CD-CQDs nanoparticles is amorphous in opposition to the crystalline state of naringenin, β-CD and naringenin-β-CD inclusion complex. Finally, antioxidant activity analyses against DPPH, ABTS+ and Fe2+ chelating, showed an enhanced antioxidant activity of the formed composite nanoparticles compared to their constituents. These results indicated that naringenin can be effectively entrapped in β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dots, forming composite nanoparticles with improved antioxidant properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Naringenin
β-cyclodextrin
Carbon quantum dots
Nanocomposites
Antioxidant

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs and NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanocomposite

Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study is supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFD0901106), the Wenzhou Major Science and Technology Project (ZN2021002) and the Ningbo “3315 series program” for high-level talents (2020B-34-G).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of naringenin (NAR) and phase solubility diagram of NAR/β-CD inclusion complex.
EE and LC of NAR-β-CD and NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanoparticles.
(a) FTIR and (b) XRD spectrum of NAR, β-CD, CQDs, NAR-β-CD-CQDs physical mixture, NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complex and NAR-CQDs.
(a) DSC and (b) TGA thermograms of NAR, β-CD, CQDs, physical mixture and inclusion complex.
DPPH radical scavenging ability (A) and IC50 (B), ABTS+ radical scavenging ability (C) and IC50 (D), Fe2+chelating ability (E) and IC50 (F) of samples.

head.ce:title: Preparation, characterization of naringenin, β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dot antioxidant nanocomposites

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Ningbo Research Institute, Zhejiang University, Ningbo 315100, China (L. Cai).

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The first two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, naringenin loaded β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dots composite nanoparticles were successfully fabricated. The results showed that incorporation of carbon quantum dots not only enhanced antioxidant activities of nanoparticles but also improved encapsulation efficiency of naringenin. Further, the formation of composite nanoparticles was confirmed by a series of characterization methods. The zeta-potential and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy data proved that electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding are dominant forces to form nanoparticles. X-Ray Diffraction experiment revealed that the material state of the formed naringenin-β-CD-CQDs nanoparticles is amorphous in opposition to the crystalline state of naringenin, β-CD and naringenin-β-CD inclusion complex. Finally, antioxidant activity analyses against DPPH, ABTS+ and Fe2+ chelating, showed an enhanced antioxidant activity of the formed composite nanoparticles compared to their constituents. These results indicated that naringenin can be effectively entrapped in β-cyclodextrin and carbon quantum dots, forming composite nanoparticles with improved antioxidant properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Naringenin (NAR) is the aglycone part of naringin, a natural flavonoid existing widely in citrus fruits involving grapefruit, lemon and orange (Papaioannou, Christoforides, & Bethanis, 2020) (Fig. 1 ). It has a multitude of biological activities including antioxidant, anti-apoptotic, anti-inflammatory, anti-tumor, anti-bacterial and so on (Memariani, Abbas, Hassan, Ahmadi, & Chabra, 2020). However, the low water solubility, easy oxidation, decomposition on light and low absorption rate in vivo greatly limited its application (Moghaddam et al., 2020). In order to address this thorny problem, researchers set out to use nanocarriers encapsulating NAR as potential delivery system. Therefore, there were an arm of materials used to wrap NAR. Among others, whey protein isolate (Yin, Fu, Cheng, Wusigale & Liang, 2020), β-casein (Li, Kembaren, Ni & Kleijn, 2021) and β-cyclodextrin (Lucas-Abellán et al., 2019) were common.

β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) is a kind of natural cyclic oligosaccharide, which is a ring-like macromolecule consisting of seven (α-1,4)-linked α-d-glucopyranose units (Zarandona, Barba, Guerrero, Caba & Maté, 2020). It is the enzymatic degradation product of starch under the action of amylase. In addition, β-CD has the structure characteristics of a truncated cone shape with a hydrophilic outer surface and a lipophilic inner cavity that, in aqueous environment, favors the inclusion of hydrophobic molecules or parts of molecules with suitable size (Abarca, Rodríguez, Guarda, Galotto, & Bruna, 2016; Aytac, Kusku, Durgun, & Uyar, 2016; Matshetshe, Parani, Manki, & Oluwafemi, 2018). Accordingly, β-CD can be used to enhance the water solubility of polyphenols, and improve their physical and chemical properties (Ficarra et al., 2002). Numerous studies have been conducted on the embedding and delivery of polyphenols by β-cyclodextrin. In a previous reported study investigating the inclusion of quercetin in β-CD, it was shown that quercetin/β-cyclodextrin inclusion complex embedded nanofibers by electrospinning could promote water solubility and antioxidant activity (Aytac, Kusku, Durgun, & Uyar, 2016). Furthermore, naringenin-loaded sulfobutylether-β-cyclodextrin/chitosan nanoparticles were also investigated. The authors prepared nanoparticles with modified β-cyclodextrin and chitosan to improve the bioavailability of naringenin (Zhang, Liu, Hu, Bai, & Zhang, 2016). Several groups have studied the complexation of β-CD and naringenin in the past 40 years. With the aim to reduce the bitter taste of naringin by complexation using β-cyclodextrin, the complex formation was proved by NMR studies (Konno, Misaki, Toda, Wada, & Yasumatsu, 1982). Moreover, the crystal structures of naringenin inclusion complexes in methylated β-CDs have been determined and extensively analyzed by Molecular Dynamics simulations in aqueous environment by Papaioannou et al. (Papaioannou, Christoforides, & Bethanis, 2020). The effect on solubilization of naringenin (ionized and unionized) upon complexation with β-CD, hydroxypropyl-β-CD and methyl β-CD was investigated by Tommasini et al. (Tommasini, Calabrò, Raneri, Ficarra, P & Ficarra, R, 2004) whereas a high, 32.5 fold, increase of naringenin solubility upon complexation with HP-β-CD was reported by Wen et al. (Wen, Liu, Yuan, Ma & Zhu, 2010). However, little attention has focused on β-CD in combination with CQDs derived from Maillard reaction of glucose and l-lysine to encapsulate polyphenols (Elaine S.M. et al., 2020; Li, Pei, & Liu, 2020; Gao et al., 2017; Pooja, Shefali, & Ki, 2019; Li, D. et al., 2018).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), first discovered by Scrivens and co-workers in 2004, are carbon-based emerging nanomaterials applied in various fields (Xu et al., 2004). Generally speaking, CQDs are discrete spherical fluorescent nanoparticles with a diameter of less than 10 nm consisting mainly of carbon, oxygen, hydrogen and/or nitrogen. Thus, there are a lot of functional groups on the surface of CQDs, which exhibit unique physical and chemical properties, such as high water solubility, strong fluorescence and good photostability (Li, Cao, Li, Yu, & Tan, 2019). In recent years, the anti-cancer drug delivery systems supported by fluorescent CQDS have attracted extensive attention. According to the differences between cancer microenvironment and normal physiological microenvironment (pH, strong reductivity, differences in enzyme expression, high reactive oxygen species, and sensitivity to acosto-photothermal), different types of CQDs are combined with anti-cancer drugs through non-covalent or covalent methods, forming a CQDS drug delivery system with various response modes (Elaine S.M. et al., 2020; Li, Pei, & Liu, 2020; Gao et al., 2017; Pooja, Shefali, & Ki, 2019). Besides, CQDs as fluorescent probes are widely applied in food safety, especially in metal ions, anions, pesticides, veterinary drugs, bacteria and functional components (Shi et al., 2018).

Maillard reaction is a thermally induced browning reaction widely used in the food industry. In general, the condensation reaction between the reducing sugar and the amino group is the first step of the Maillard reaction. This reaction is followed by an advanced stage in which a complex series of reactions, including dehydration, rearrangement, isomerization, and condensation occurred leading to the formation of brown nitrogen-containing polymers known as “melanoids”. It is worth noted that the reactions occurring in the advanced stages of the Maillard reaction, especially the condensation reactions, were similar to the hydrothermal synthesis process of CQDs derived from molecular precursors, such as citric acid and ammonia solutions. CQDS were thought to be related to the Maillard Reaction and they have recently been used to make tunable multicolor displays (Li, D. et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2016). Li, D. et al. (2018) synthesized CQDS derived from Maillard reaction of glucose and lysine and they have investigated the antioxidant activity of CQDs. The results showed that CQDs exhibit scavenging activity against DPPH and hydroxyl radicals (Li, D. et al., 2018). Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2019) have also investigated CQDs prepared from baked lamb showing scavenging capability to free radicals, and cell protection from oxidative damage in vitro.

In this study, NAR is encapsulated by β-CD and CQDs forming composite nanoparticles. β-CD can encapsulate NAR to improve its solubility and bioavailability, while carbon quantum dots can improve its antioxidant performance. NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanocomposite is characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Calorimetry (DSC) and thermogravimetry (TGA). Further, the antioxidant activity of NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanocomposite is studied by DPPH free radical scavenging ability, ABTS+ free radical scavenging ability and Fe2+chelating ability. The innovation of this work is based on the use of CQDs to assist the embedding of NAR in β-CD. It is proved that CQDs not only improve the embedding rate but also lead to the formation of composite nanoparticles with enhanced antioxidant activity.

NAR (97%) was purchased from Shanghai Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd.; β-CD (>97%) was purchased from Beijing Suo Laibao Technology Co., Ltd.; Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by glucose and lysine. Other analytical grade reagents were all from Beijing Suo Laibao Technology Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs and NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanocomposite

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were generated by Maillard reaction (Li, D. et al., 2018). First, 0.6 g glucose and 0.6 g l-lysine were dissolved in 40 mL deionized water in an ultrasonic processor (KQ-400KDE, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co., Ltd., Kunshan, Jiangsu, China) to form solution which was heated in the microwave for 30 min. During this process, the color of the solution changed from colorless to light yellow, and eventually solid was formed indicating the formation of CQDs. Then, prepared CQDs were dissolved in a small amount of deionized water and centrifuged (10000 r/min, 10 min) to remove solid precipitate obtaining supernatant which was further filtered by disposable needle filter (0.22 μm hydrophilic membrane). Finally, supernatant was placed in a dialysis bag and dialyzed in deionized water for 24 h. After that, supernatant was transferred to a freeze dryer (Free Zone 2.5, Labconco Corporation, Kansas city, Missouri, USA) and dried for one day (−40 °C) to obtain solid pure CQDs. Dried CQDs were kept at 4 °C for further use.

Phase solubility experiment was performed based on Higuchi and Connors method (Higuchi, & Connors, 1965). First, excessive naringenin was dissolved in 20 mL β-CD aqueous solution with concentrations of 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12 and 14 mM, respectively. After mixing, the solution was placed in dark and stirred (DF-101S, Zhengzhou Changcheng Branch Industry and Trade Co., Ltd, Henan, China) at room temperature for 48 h. Then, solution was filtered by a 0.45 μm hydrophilic membrane and centrifuged at 12, 000 rpm at 4 °C for 20 min. Finally, 1 mL filtrate was diluted with deionized water10 times. The content of naringenin in supernatant was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Unico Instrument Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China) at 288 nm. All experiments were repeated three times. According to the linear part of the phase solubility curve, the stability constant (Kc) was calculated using the Higuchi-Connors equation.
(1)
K c =
S l o p e
S 0 × ( 1 - S l o p e )
S0: the solubility of NAR without β-CD

The solubilizing efficiency of β-CD was evaluated by the complexation efficiency (CE). CE is the concentration ratio of complex to free β-CD calculated from the slope of the phase solubility diagram according to the following equation:
(2)
C E = S 0 × K c =
S l o p e
1 - S l o p e

The NAR-β-CD inclusion complex was obtained by the co-precipitation method with some modifications. NAR (0.1 g) was mixed with 20 mL methanol in a beaker which was placed in a water bath at 50 °C to obtain transparent solution. An isomolar quantity of β-CD (0.41 g) was added into 30 mL deionized water and placed in a water bath at 60 °C. Naringenin solution was slowly dropped into β-CD solution maintaining 50 °C. The mixed solution was stirred for 1.5 h at constant temperature of 60 °C. In order to remove methanol, the mixture solution was placed at 80 °C and heated for 3 h with stirring continuously. Subsequently, the solution was stirred for 5 h and left to sit overnight. The inclusion complex of NAR-β-CD was obtained by freeze-drying the solution. Dried NAR-β-CD inclusion complexes were kept at 4 °C for further use.

CQDS (100 mL, 1 mg/mL) solution was mixed with NAR-β-CD solution (100 mL, 1 mg/mL). NAR-β-CD-CQDs solution was freeze-dried after magnetic stirring in the dark for 24 h. Dried NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complexes were kept at 4 °C for further use.
To prepare physical mixture of NAR-β-CD-CQDs, 1 mg NAR, 4 mg β-CD and 5 mg CQDs were mixed in mortar. The NAR-β-CD-CQDs physical mixture was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The total amount of NAR encapsulated in colloidal particles was determined by a spectrophotometer as described earlier (Li, D. et al., 2018) with some modifications. Briefly, 3 mL freshly fabricated sample was centrifuged (Legend Micro21R, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts) at 10,000 g at 4 °C for 30 min. The supernatant was extracted and diluted with ethyl alcohol absolute to 0.1 mg/ml. Then the absorbance of the supernatant was measured at 288 nm by UV–Vis spectrophotometer to evaluate EE and LC according to the equations:
(3)
E E
( % )
=
e n c a p s u l a t e d
N A R ( m g )
t o t a l
N A R
( m g )
× 100
(4)
L C
( % )
=
e n c a p s u l a t e d
N A R ( m g )
w e i g h t
o f
N A R - β - C D - C Q D s
n a n o c o m p o s i t e
× 100

The zeta-potential and particle size were measured using a nanoparticle size analyzer (90Plus, Brookhaven Instruments Corporation, Holtsville NY, USA). The measurements were obtained in Milli-Q water as dispersant with 0.1 mg/mL sample concentration, at room temperature (25 °C) and under a fixed angle of 90 °C.

FTIR spectra of NAR, β-CD, CQDs, NAR-CQDs, NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complex and NAR-β-CD-CQDs physical mixture were obtained using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Scimitar 2000, Agilent Technologies Inc., Santa Clara, California, USA). Sample (1 mg) and dried KBr (100 mg) were ground into uniform powder in an agate mortar, then the sample was loaded into sheet and put into sample chamber for scanning. The wavenumber scan range was 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 2 cm−1. The characteristic peaks were recorded for different samples (Li et al., 2019). The collected data were analyzed by OMNIC 8.2 (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA).

The powder diffraction patterns of NAR, β-CD, CQDs, NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complex and NAR-β-CD-CQDs physical mixture were obtained by the Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffraction instrument (Rigaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan). The scan range was from 5° to 60°(2θ) with a speed of 4°/min using Cu Kɑ radiation. The X-ray generator’s tube current and operating voltage were 40 mA and 40 kV respectively.

DSC analyses of NAR, β-CD, CQDs, NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complex and NAR-β-CD-CQDs physical mixture were conducted according to previous study (Pan, & Zhong, 2016). The heat shrinkage temperature and thermal enthalpy of different samples were measured using a differential scanning calorimeter (Q2000, TA Instruments, New Castle, USA). About 6 mg sample was weighed into a DSC aluminum pan, and sealed with a lid. The temperature heated from 20 °C to 270 °C with a rate of 10 °C/min and an empty aluminum pan was used as a reference. The denaturation temperature and converted enthalpy were analyzed by the TA Universal Analysis software (Q2000, TA Instruments, New Castle, USA).

Thermogravimetric analyses of NAR, β-CD, CQDs inclusion complex and physical mixture were performed by using a STA 449 F5 Jupiter thermal analysis instrument (NETZSCH, Germany). Each sample was scanned from 50 °C to 950 °C at a constant rate of 10 °C/min. All measurements were conducted under nitrogen atmosphere.

The scavenging ability of NAR, β-CD, CQDs, NAR-β-CD, NAR-CQDs and NAR-β-CD-CQDs against DPPH was carried out as previously reported for CQDs by Li et al. (Li, H. et al., 2018). Briefly, CQDs solutions (1 mg/ml) were diluted into different concentrations (0.2 mg/mL, 0.4 mg/mL, 0.6 mg/mL, 0.8 mg/mL and 1.0 mg/mL). Then, 2 mL of each prepared samples were mixed with 2 mL DPPH (0.04 g/L) anhydrous ethanol (experimental group). 2.0 mL sample was added to 2.0 mL anhydrous ethanol (control group). 2.0 mL DPPH anhydrous ethanol was added to 2 mL deionized water (blank group). All the above three groups were shaken evenly, and reacted for 60 min at room temperature. Then the absorption value was measured at 517 nm. A similar protocol was followed for NAR, β-CD, NAR-β-CD, NAR-CQDs and NAR-β-CD-CQDs samples. Trolox was used as a reference standard. DPPH radical scavenging capacity was calculated by the following equation:
(5)
D P P H
r a d i c a l
s c a v e n g i n g
a c t i v i t y
( % )
= 1 -
A0 - A1
A2
× 100
where A0 is the absorption of experimental group. A1 is the absorption of control group. A2 is the absorption of blank group. Each sample was repeated three times.

The ABTS+ scavenging experiment was based on one described previously by Huang et al. (Huang et al, 2016). Briefly, CQDs solutions (1 mg/ml) were diluted into different concentrations (0.2 mg/mL, 0.4 mg/mL, 0.6 mg/mL, 0.8 mg/mL and 1.0 mg mL). ABTS+ solution was prepared by mixing 7 mmol/L ABTS anhydrous ethanol solution with 2.45 mmol/L potassium persulfate (K2S2O4) solution at 1:1 (v/v) keeping it at room temperature for 16 h in the dark. ABTS+ solution was diluted with anhydrous ethanol until the absorbance value was 0.70 (±0.02) cm−1 at 734 nm. Three milliliters prepared samples were mixed with 1 mL ABTS+ solution (experimental group). Three milliliters prepared samples were mixed with 1 mL anhydrous ethanol (control group). Three milliliters deionized water were mixed with 1 mL ABTS+ solution (blank group). All the above three groups were reacted for 30 min at room temperature in the dark. Then the absorption value was measured at 734 nm. The same procedure was followed for NAR, β-CD, NAR-β-CD, NAR-CQDs and NAR-β-CD-CQDs. Trolox was used as a reference standard. ABTS+ radical scavenging capacity of samples was calculated by the following equation:
(6)
A B T S +
r a d i c a l
s c a v e n g i n g
a c t i v i t y
( % )
= 1 -
A0 - A1
A2
× 100
where A0 is the absorption of experimental group. A1 is the absorption of control group. A2 is the absorption of blank group. Each sample was repeated three times.

The Fe2+-chelating capacity was investigated as previously described by Li et al. (Li, D. et al., 2018) with minor modifications. 500 µL of different concentrations (0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 and 1 mg/mL) CQDS solutions、25 µL FeCl2 (2 mM) solution and 500 µL distilled water were mixed, respectively. After reaction for 5 min, 50 µL Ferrozine (5 mM) was added and reacted at room temperature for 5 min. The absorbance of the supernatant was determined at 562 nm after centrifugation (2000 rpm) for 10 min. The same steps were followed for NAR, β-CD, NAR-β-CD, NAR-CQDs and NAR-β-CD-CQDs. EDTA-2Na was used as a reference standard. The ability of each sample to chelate Fe2+ was calculated as follows:
(7)
F e 2+
C h e l a t i n g
a b i l i t y
( % )
= 1 -
A0 - A1
A2
× 100
where A0 is the absorption of experimental group. A1 is the absorption of control group. A2 is the absorption of blank group. Each sample was repeated three times.

Univariate analysis of variance (ANOVA) analysis of all quantitative data (P < 0.05) was followed by the Duncan multiple range test, expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD) (SPSS 22.0, SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA). All figures were drawn using OriginPro 9.0 (OriginLab Co., Northampton, MA, USA). The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

As shown in the phase solubility diagram of NAR/β-CD inclusion complex (Fig. 1), the solubility of NAR increases linearly with increase of cyclodextrin concentration. The phase-solubility profile is of AL type according to the theory of Higuchi and Connors (Higuchi, & Connors, 1965), suggesting a 1:1 stoichiometry for the NAR inclusion complex in β-CD. The Kc of complex is 473 M−1 (The slope is 0.0153 and the S0 is 3.28 × 10−5 M) in agreement with previous study (Sangpheak et al., 2015), indicating that β-CD molecules can encapsulate NAR and increase the solubility of NAR to a certain extent. The CE value, representing the solubilization efficiency of β-CD to NAR, is of 0.015. Native β-CD is not the best host for NAR (modified β-CD have higher binding affinity to NAR (Lucas-Abellán et al., 2019)), however it is the safer cyclodextrin for food applications.

The EE and LC of the prepared NAR inclusion complexes are presented in Fig. 2 . The EE of NAR-β-CD complex and NAR-β-CD-CQDs complex is 33.5 % and 56.9 %, respectively. The EE of NAR-β-CD-CQDs complex is higher than NAR-β-CD complex, which is attributed to the fact that the noncovalent interactions among NAR, β-CD and CQDs might improve the EE of NAR (Dai et al., 2017). On the other hand, the LC of NAR-β-CD is higher than that of NAR-β-CD-CQDs, as the mass of NAR-β-CD-CQDs is significantly larger than NAR-β-CD. This is consistent with previous study (Wei et al., 2019).

Zeta-potential and particle size values of samples are shown in Fig. 3 . The particle sizes of β-CD and NAR are 326.73 ± 149.43 nm and 286.87 ± 180.96 nm, respectively. When they combine together, the size is slightly elevated to 360.8 ± 28.86 nm (NAR/β-CD). This phenomenon might be attributed to the aggregation tendency of the formed inclusion complexes. The large particle size and high SD (Standard Deviation) for β-CD suggest aggregation. It is noteworthy that the SD of the complex is much smaller showing lower polydispersity. Besides, the addition of CQDs makes the size of the nanoparticles decrease to 196.73 ± 21.87 nm (NAR/β-CD/CQDs). This can be explained by the fact that the negative charge of CQDs (−4.71 ± 0.56 mV) decreases NAR/β-CD/CQDs charge (−10.13 ± 3.16 mV) providing a sufficient electrostatic repulsion against aggregation. Interestingly, NAR/CQDs and β-CD/CQDs are 275.9 ± 77.86 nm and 544.07 ± 122.54 nm, which are smaller than CQDs (952.33 ± 95.78 nm). This is attributed to the fact that the negative charge of NAR (−5.48 ± 2.79 mV) and β-CD (−5.22 ± 4.31 mV) decrease NAR/CQDs (−7.05 ± 3.08 mV) and β-CD/CQDs (−4.81 ± 2.59 mV) charge (Wei et al., 2019). While the complexation of NAR either with β-CD or with CQDs does not significantly affect the aggregation of NAR, the aggregation of CQDs is hindered in a large extent by both NAR and β-CD when used separately and show synergy when applied together.

The potential inter-molecular interactions among NAR, β-CD, and CQDs constituting the nanoparticles are investigated by FTIR (Fig. 4 a). The band in 3100–3500 cm−1 is due to the OH stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group. In the native spectra of NAR, β-CD and CQDs, the characteristic peaks of hydrogen bonds are at 3122.7, 3383.1 and 3435.2 cm−1 respectively. Obviously, the OH stretching bands were shifted to 3402.4 cm−1 in the spectrum of NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complex, thereby implying the formation of hydrogen bonds among NAR, β-CD and CQDs. The above results are well consistent with a previous report on zein nanoparticles (Dai et al., 2017). The spectrum for NAR has peaks at 1633, 1462 and 1250 cm−1, representing the stretching vibration of CO on the pyrone, the stretching vibration of CC on the benzene ring and the stretching vibration of COC, respectively (Zhao et al., 2018). The primary characteristic peaks of β-CD are 2926 cm−1 (CH stretching)、1645 cm−1 (HOH bending) and 1159 cm−1 (CO stretching) (Matshetshe, Parani, Manki, & Oluwafemi, 2018). Additionally, the spectrum of CQDs also shows two characteristic peaks at 1633.7 and 1411.8 cm−1, which are associated with the stretching of the CO and CH (Li, Cao, Li, Yu, & Tan, 2019). Compared to NAR and CQDs, the CO stretching vibration peak in the inclusion complex shifts from 1633.7 cm−1 to 1629.8 cm−1, while the peak in the physical mixture still remain at1633.7 cm−1, which might be attributed to the incorporation of β-CD. The spectrum of NAR-CQDs is similar to that of physical mixture. Similar peaks appear at 3367.7 cm-1and 1633.7 cm−1 in both spectra, whereas a more pronounced peak at 1026.1 cm−1 appears in the physical mixture spectrum, which can be attributed to the presence of β-CD. This phenomenon indicate that NAR is simply attached to CQDs. Moreover, a new peak is found in the spectrum of inclusion complex at 1035.7 cm−1 indicating the formation of NAR-β-CD-CQDs complex. The spectrum of the physical mixtures is almost a simple superposition of naringenin、β-CD and CQDs and no new characteristic absorption peak is generated. These results confirm that the complex is successfully formed.

The powder-XRD patterns of the examined samples are shown in Fig. 4b. As seen from XRD diffractograms, the major sharp peaks at 9°, 10.7°, 12.5°, 18.9°, 20.7°, 22.7°, 27°, 35° of β-CD and 10.7°, 15.7°, 18°, 20.3°, 22.2°, 25.3° of NAR, indicating that pure β-CD and NAR are in the crystalline state (Abarca, Rodríguez, Guarda, Galotto, & Bruna, 2016). On the contrary, CQDs have a broad peak at 2θ of 21.2°, rather than sharp peaks, suggesting they are in the amorphous form. Noteworthy, crystallization peaks of β-CD and NAR are detected in the XRD pattern of the physical mixture, which indicates no change in the crystallization form of β-CD and NAR in physical mixture. Similar phenomena had been also observed by other researchers for other systems (Zhu et al., 2016). On the other hand, the XRD diffractogram of the inclusion complex shows tiny characteristic crystalline peaks of β-CD while characteristic peaks of NAR disappear, indicating the complex formation. Further, the peak intensities of NAR-β-CD-CQDs composite nanoparticles at the diffraction angles of 10.7° and 20.7° are significantly reduced,
compared to those observed in the diffractogram of plain β-CD, thus it can be said that NAR-β-CD-CQDs are rather amorphous.

Thermal properties of samples are investigated by DSC which is a fast and dependable technique for inclusion complex characterization. Fig. 5 a shows the DSC thermograms for NAR, β-CD, CQDs, NAR-β-CD-CQDs physical mixture and NAR-β-CD-CQDs inclusion complex. CQDs exhibit a broad endothermic peak at 125 °C. This characteristic endotherm can be associated with the evaporation of bound water from the CQDs during the degradation. In the thermogram curve of NAR, a great sharp endothermic peak is observed around 253 °C, which is ascribed to the melting of crystalline NAR. Zhang et al. (2016) reported that the melting point of NAR was observed around 258 °C. However, this melting peak was not observed in the inclusion complex thermogram curve. A similar phenomenon had been reported by Sun et al. (2017) who confirmed that the crystalline curcumin was converted to the amorphous state after being entrapped in zein-shellac composite colloidal particles. Moreover, a broad endothermic peak at 140 °C appears in the curve of inclusion complex. This phenomenon might be attributed to that the hydrophobic NAR enters into the β-CD cavity while hydrophilic CQDs coat the exterior rims of the host β-CDs. Therefore, sharp peak of NAR and β-CD can’t be observed in the DSC curve of inclusion complex, and only a wide peak similar to the curve of CQDs is observed. In the physical mixture profile, there are two sharp endothermic peaks around 190 °C and 250 °C. The endothermic peak of 190 °C can explain the shift of the endo peak, from the 95 °C observed in the thermogram of β-CD to 190 °C observed in the thermogram of the physical mixture. The endothermic peak of about 250 °C observed in β-CD and NAR thermograms, indicates the crystalline state decomposition of β-CD and NAR respectively. A similar, though less intense endothermic peak at 250 °C is observed in the thermogram of the physical mixture confirming that β-CD and NAR are in the crystalline form in the physical mixture, as also shown by the XRD experiment (Fig. 4). The lower heat flow peak compared to the pure form is related to the smaller amount of β-CD and NAR in the physical mixture. Our results are in good accordance with the results previously reported by Doost, Muhammad, Stevens, Dewettinck, & Meeren (2018) on quercetin loaded almond gum-shellac nanoparticles.

The thermal properties of the examined samples, indicating also aspects of the inclusion complex formation, were further analyzed by the TGA method as previously described by Zhu et al. (Zhu et al., 2016). Fig. 5b shows the weight loss (TG) thermograms of NAR、β-CD、CQDs、physical mixture and inclusion complex. CQDs have only one weight loss zone (72%) from 30 °C (initial temperature) to about 476 °C, associating with the water evaporation. For β-CD, three stages can be distinguished during the heating process of the sample. The first stage is between 30 °C to 105 °C where a slight weight loss is observed. This is due to the loss of adsorbed water and water of crystallization. The mass loss at this stage is 11.47% of total weight. The second stage, ranging from 105 to 350 °C is characterized by a stable curve in the range of approximately 105–300 °C and a rapid curve fall-off in the range of 300–350 °C. The latter indicates a major weight loss, which corresponds to the β-CD thermal degradation. The mass loss observed at this second stage is 64.3% of total weight. A third stage is observed from 350 °C to the final temperature (950 °C) of the experiment. The solid residuals continuously decompose at a very slow rate as it is indicated by the slowly continuous loss of weight shown in the β-CD TG curve. The mass loss of the third stage is 10.6% of total weight. The residue yield of β-CD is 13.63%. The curve of NAR has weight reduction between 30 °C to 460 °C and the mass loss is 71.17%. Furthermore, it can be seen that the curve of the physical mixture follows that of NAR indicating a similar weight loss behavior. The thermogram of the inclusion complex indicates that it is relatively stable between 30 and 300 °C, whereas a weight loss of 45.42% appears from 300 to 395 °C. It is obvious that the thermal stability of NAR is significantly enhanced in the inclusion complex.

The antioxidant activity is tested to evaluate the antioxidant capacity of nanoparticle. Antioxidants scavenging hydroxyl radicals occurs in two ways: they can react directly with hydroxyl radicals (DPPH free radical, ABTS+ free radical) or indirectly inhibit hydroxyl radical formation by chelating metal ions that are involved in the formation of hydroxyl radicals (Li, D. et al., 2018). The antioxidant activities of different samples are determined using DPPH free radical scavenging, ABTS+ free radical scavenging ability and Fe2+chelating ability. As shown in Fig. 6 A and 6B, the antioxidant activity is improved with the increase of concentration of each sample. However, the antioxidant activity of all the examined samples has been found to be lower than that of Trolox. Compared with pure NAR (IC50 = 0.75 mg/ml), the DPPH antioxidant activity increases in presence of β-CD (IC50 = 0.58 mg/ml), which can be ascribed to the inclusion of the hydrophobic NAR in β-CDs that increases NAR dispersion in the water system and allows a better contact of NAR with free radicals (Huang et al., 2016). This phenomenon has been also described in a previous study of Liu et al. (Liu, Ying, Cai, & Le, 2017) in which, by evaluating DPPH radical scavenging activity of ovalbumin, curcumin and their complex, an improved antioxidant activity of curcumin by adding ovalbumin is reported. Besides, the antioxidant activity of NAR-β-CD-CQDs (IC50 = 0.39 mg/ml) is significantly higher (p＜0.05) than NAR-CQDs (IC50 = 0.42 mg/ml). Further, NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanocomposites have higher antioxidant activity than NAR-β-CD inclusion complexes and NAR. This is related to the fact that CQDs (IC50 = 1 mg/ml) have certain antioxidant activity by themselves and their presence undoubtedly increases the antioxidant activity of the NAR-β-CD inclusion complexes (Li, D. et al., 2018). The results show that β-CD and CQDs can enhance the antioxidant activity of NAR.

In Fig. 6C and 6D, it is shown that the antioxidant activity of all the examined samples has been found to be lower than that of Trolox. NAR-β-CD-CQDs (IC50 = 0.38 mg/ml) nanocomposites exhibit stronger ABTS+ scavenging ability than NAR-β-CD (IC50 = 0.42 mg/ml) inclusion complexes and NAR (IC50 = 0.62 mg/ml). The results are in accordance with DPPH.

Metal ions (such as iron and copper) act as catalysts in the process of free radical oxidation. Metal ion chelation can greatly reduce the catalytic effect of metal ions avoiding the generation of free radicals. Therefore, the determination of metal chelating activity is a commonly used method to evaluate the oxidation resistance of samples. Ferrozine is able to form a purplish red chelate with Fe2+.When other competitive chelators are present the color becomes lighter. Therefore, the chelating ability of Fe2+ can be evaluated by the change of chelate color. In Fig. 6E and 6F, it is shown that the Fe2+ chelating ability is improved with the increase of concentration of each sample. However, in every case, the chelating ability of the examined samples is found to be lower than that of EDTA-2Na (IC50 = 0.24 mg/ml). Besides, at the same concentration, chelating ability of NAR-β-CD-CQDs (IC50 = 0.37 mg/ml) nanocomposites is higher than NAR-β-CD (IC50 = 0.5 mg/ml) and NAR (IC50 = 0.62 mg/ml). This is attributed to that CQDs can chelate metal ions (Li, D. et al., 2018). Further, the IC50 of NAR-CQDs towards Fe2+chelating ability is determined to be 0.42 mg/ml. This result may show that CQDs are simply mixed with NAR, not forming any kind of complex with it. Therefore, NAR-CQDs are less capable of chelating metal ions than NAR-β-CD-CQDs.

In the present study, NAR-β-CD-CQDs nanocomposites are prepared to improve the stability and bioavailability of NAR and a series of methods (FTIR, XRD, DSC and TGA) are used to characterize the nanoparticles. The NAR-β-CD-CQDs supramolecular systems are formed by non-covalent interactions, offering improvement of NAR physicochemical properties as water solubility, thermal stability and its slow release in biological systems. Additionally, compared to NAR-β-CD inclusion complex, incorporation of CQDs not only improves encapsulation efficiency of NAR from 33.5% to 56.9% but also enhances antioxidant activities of nanoparticles. This work is of great potential for complex nanoparticles as delivery vehicle for bioactive compounds.

Yan Liang: Manuscript writing; Design of experimental Program; Measurements of all experimental data, Result analysis. Dongyuan Hou: Manuscript writing; Provide Suggestions for experimental scheme modification. Zan Ni: Design of experimental Program; Modification of experimental scheme. Minjie Cao: Manuscript language modification; Provide Suggestions for experimental scheme modification. Luyun Cai: Provide fund support; Design of experimental Program; Manuscript writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFD0901106), the Wenzhou Major Science and Technology Project (ZN2021002) and the Ningbo “3315 series program” for high-level talents (2020B-34-G).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhang et al., 2016

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation and evaluation of naringenin-loaded sulfobutylether-β-cyclodextrin/chitosan nanoparticles for ocular drug delivery
